origin: A passage from an early Christian scripture (the Gospel of John), likely spoken by Jesus to his disciples near the end of the Farewell Discourse (John 14–17). The context is reassurance before impending hardship.
author: Most likely the author/tradition attributed to the Gospel of John (the “Johannine” author/evangelist), commonly identified as John the Apostle or a Johannine community figure.
thoughts: The author’s (or Jesus’ narrated) motivation appears to be pastoral encouragement: acknowledging that believers will experience distress in the “world,” while grounding courage in the claim that Jesus has already overcome the world. The perspective is theological and confident—suffering is real, but not ultimate; peace comes “in” Jesus.
audience: First-century Christian believers (especially disciples/early church readers) who are facing persecution, social pressure, or spiritual trials and need reassurance and perseverance.
written_when: Late first century CE (commonly dated to roughly AD 90–100), based on scholarly estimates for the Gospel of John’s composition.
